commonMistakes: Suppressing the dependency linter instead of fixing the data flow.
Using an Effect for render-time derivation.
Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain effect decision process using a new example and justify the result.
Explain dependency management using a new example and justify the result.
Explain avoiding unnecessary effects using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Effect decision process** → **Dependency management** → **Avoiding unnecessary effects**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect decision process.
choices.label: Start from the data model and static component tree, identify minimal state, choose its owner, and add upward communication through callbacks.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect decision process.
choices.label: useCallback caches a function definition when function identity matters to a consumer or dependency.

choices.feedback: Effect decision process is best understood as follows: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.
choices.label: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect decision process.
choices.label: UI state describes current interaction. Remote state is server-owned and adds asynchronous freshness, caching, and synchronization concerns.

choices.feedback: Dependency management is best understood as follows: Keep dependencies complete and stabilize values only where identity is genuinely required.

choices.feedback: Avoiding unnecessary effects is best understood as follows: Do not use Effects for render-time derivation, click logic, or duplicate-state synchronization.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
choices.label: Apply Effect decision process deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.
questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Suppressing the dependency linter instead of fixing the data flow.”?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Effect decision process, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Avoid `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices.
choices.label: Use `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices in every file because more abstraction is always better.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices?

sections.body: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.

sections.body: Keep dependencies complete and stabilize values only where identity is genuinely required.
sections.title: Dependency management

summary: `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices connects Effect decision process, Dependency management, Avoiding unnecessary effects.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: `useEffect` Rules and Best Practices

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use `useeffect` rules and best practices without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Performance and Advanced Effects** module.
